ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for chicken tikka masala; A0CKP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chicken tikka masala is approximately 350 g, comprising marinated grilled chicken pieces in a rich spiced tomato-cream gravy, usually served with rice or naan bread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Tikka Masala — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for restaurant-style chicken tikka masala as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chicken tikka masala' (FDC ID 2103689, USDA Branded Foods / SR Legacy composite) and cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) values for chicken curry with gravy. Per-100g values represent the combined dish (chicken plus sauce) as typically served. Values are consistent across both sources within ±5% for core macros.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry (GI ~35) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken tikka masala was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 35 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: chicken curry with tomato-based sauce (GI approximately 30–40, low category), consistent with the low carbohydrate content and high protein and fat content of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 21.7) / 100 ≈ 7.6, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 6.2 × 3.5 = 21.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1330 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of chicken tikka masala may contribute approximately 1330 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~6.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken tikka masala has a low glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 7.6 per restaurant serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g) and sodium (~380 mg per 100 g) from cream and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and oil used in chicken tikka masala contribute moderate saturated fat (~10.2 g per restaurant serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose preparations with reduced cream, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tikka Masala is a popular Indian curry dish consisting of marinated chicken pieces — typically breast or thigh — that are first grilled or roasted (tikka style) and then simmered in a rich, mildly spiced sauce made from tomatoes, onions, cream or yoghurt, and a blend of aromatic spices including garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger, and garlic. The dish is characterised by its vibrant orange-red colour, creamy texture, and a balanced flavour profile that combines mild heat, tanginess from tomatoes, and richness from dairy. It is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Maharani Table at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, chicken tikka masala is a protein-rich dish (~12.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (~7.8 g per 100 g, of which ~2.9 g is saturated) and relatively low carbohydrate content (~6.2 g per 100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 35. The sodium content is notable (~380 mg per 100 g), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is generally halal and suitable for non-vegetarians. It is commonly served with basmati rice, naan bread, or roti, and is a staple of North Indian cuisine that has become globally popular. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI and high protein content make it a favourable main-course option when portion size is managed and high-GI accompaniments are limited.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry precisely matches restaurant-style chicken tikka masala as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central and cross-validated with ICMR-NIN data for chicken curry; values are internally consistent and plausible but represent an estimate rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable chicken curry entries.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN source data for this dish type. All other micronutrient values with -1 sentinel are not applicable here as they have been populated from available source data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central composite data for chicken tikka masala and are considered reliable estimates. The food is classified as Indian cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in all five mandatory languages for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). GI category is low (GI = 35, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving = (35 × 21.7) / 100 ≈ 7.6 g, based on a 350 g serving containing approximately 21.7 g carbohydrate. The input record listed cuisine as 'Singapore' reflecting the serving context; region_of_origin is correctly set to India. Restaurant source (Maharani Table, Boat Quay) is preserved as contextual metadata only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
